annotations.label: Method of Lagrange multipliers

anchor.snippet: the method of Lagrange multipliers is a strategy for finding the local maxima and minima of a function subject to equation constraints

annotations.note: The method as a strategy is not named, but its core theorem is formalized in the LagrangeMultipliers file.

annotations.label: Lagrangian function (general)

anchor.snippet: the Lagrangian is defined as

annotations.note: Mathlib's Lagrange multiplier theorem does not introduce a named Lagrangian function L(x,λ).

annotations.label: Lagrange multiplier

anchor.snippet: is called the Lagrange multiplier

annotations.note: Multipliers Λ appear inside an existential statement but there is no standalone definition.

annotations.label: Lagrangian (dot product case)

anchor.snippet: where the inner product is defined as the dot product , the Lagrangian is

annotations.note: No dot-product Lagrangian is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Stationary point characterization

anchor.snippet: in order to find the maximum or minimum of a function

annotations.note: Mathlib gives the linear-dependence/multiplier statement at a constrained local extremum, but not in the form ∇L = 0.

annotations.label: Solution is a saddle point of the Lagrangian

anchor.snippet: The solution corresponding to the original constrained optimization is always a saddle point of the Lagrangian function

annotations.note: No saddle-point characterization of the Lagrangian is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lagrange multiplier theorem

anchor.snippet: The following is known as the Lagrange multiplier theorem

annotations.note: The multi-constraint Lagrange multiplier theorem is stated in this declaration.

annotations.label: Gradient as linear combination of constraint gradients

anchor.snippet: The Lagrange multiplier theorem states that at any local maximum (or minimum) of the function evaluated under the equality constraints

annotations.note: The conclusion (∑ Λ i • f' i) + Λ₀ • φ' = 0 is exactly the linear-combination statement.

annotations.label: Lagrange function (single constraint)

anchor.snippet: called a Lagrange multiplier (or Lagrange undetermined multiplier ) and study the Lagrange function

annotations.note: No definition of the single-constraint Lagrange function L(x,λ) = f(x) - λ g(x) exists in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: called a Lagrange multiplier (or Lagrange undetermined multiplier ) and study the Lagrange function

anchor.snippet: If [MATH] is a maximum of [MATH] for the original constrained problem

annotations.label: Constrained extrema as critical points of Lagrangian

anchor.snippet: The constrained extrema of f are critical points of the Lagrangian

annotations.note: Mathlib gives the derivative-vanishing condition implicitly via the multiplier statement, but never names the Lagrangian as such.

annotations.label: Multiple constraints method

anchor.snippet: The method of Lagrange multipliers can be extended to solve problems with multiple constraints using a similar argument

annotations.note: The finite-family version handles the multiple-constraints case.

annotations.label: Constraint qualification (multiple constraints)

anchor.snippet: The constraint qualification assumption when there are multiple constraints is that the constraint gradients at the relevant point are linearly independent

annotations.note: Linear (in)dependence of constraint derivatives appears as the conclusion, but no separate `ConstraintQualification` predicate is defined.

annotations.label: Stationarity on a manifold (single constraint)

anchor.snippet: Stationarity for the restriction

annotations.note: Mathlib uses the level-set formulation rather than a manifold restriction, so the manifold-flavored statement is not literally present.

annotations.label: Stationarity on a manifold (multiple constraints)

anchor.snippet: is a stationary point of

annotations.note: Same: the multi-constraint theorem is in level-set form, not as restriction to a submanifold.

annotations.label: Lagrange multiplier as rate of change

anchor.snippet: λ k is the rate of change of the quantity being optimized as a function of the constraint parameter

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize the sensitivity interpretation of λ.

annotations.label: Envelope theorem interpretation

anchor.snippet: by the envelope theorem the optimal value of a Lagrange multiplier has an interpretation as the marginal effect

annotations.note: The envelope theorem is not in Mathlib (grep found no matches).

anchors.snippet: by the envelope theorem the optimal value of a Lagrange multiplier has an interpretation as the marginal effect

annotations.label: Sufficient conditions via bordered Hessian

anchor.snippet: Sufficient conditions for a constrained local maximum or minimum can be stated in terms of a sequence of principal minors

annotations.note: The bordered Hessian / principal minors second-order test is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Economics application

anchor.snippet: consumer behavior is implemented as utility maximization and firm behavior as profit maximization

annotations.note: Economic applications are out of scope of Mathlib.

annotations.label: Power systems application

anchor.snippet: Methods based on Lagrange multipliers have applications in power systems

annotations.note: Power-systems applications are out of scope of Mathlib.

annotations.label: Safe reinforcement learning application

anchor.snippet: The method of Lagrange multipliers applies to constrained Markov decision processes

annotations.note: Constrained MDPs / safe RL are not formalized in Mathlib.
